plan.contextChunk:
\end{bmatrix}
W_Y 
\end{aligned}$$ 

In this case, the focal length $f$ will have a strong
effect on the appearance of the motion field. For very large $f$, we can
approximate the 2D motion field by $\dot{x} \approx f W_Y$ and
$\dot{y} \approx 0$. The resulting motion field is approximately
constant across the entire image and looks like lateral translation
motion. For very small $f$, the motion field will be similar to the one
produced by a homography and it will be very different to a lateral
camera motion. The flows shown in @fig-motion_wy_f03_f1_f3 correspond
to $f=1/3$, $f=1$, and $f=3$.

![Motion flows corresponding to a rotation around the 
$Y$-axis. (left) $f = 1/3$. (middle) $f = 1$. (right) $f = 3$.](figures/optical_flow/camera_motion_field_rotation_three_f.png){width="100%" #fig-motion_wy_f03_f1_f3}

Camera rotation around the $Y$-axis does not inform about the scene
structure, but it informs about the camera parameters. The magnitude of
$W_Y$ only affects the scaling of the motion vectors, but it does not
change their orientation.

In the case of camera rotation, for a large angle (or a large
$\Delta t$), the relationship between the image at time $t$ and the
image at time $t+\Delta t$ is a homography.

### Motion under Varying Focal Length

Before moving into motion estimation, let's consider one final scenario:
a static camera observing a static scene, but the focal length changes
over time. What will the motion field be? In this setting, despite that

elements: Grid of 13x13 optical flow vector arrows mapped on the 2D image plane
Outer boundary square representing the image sensor limits
3D camera model at the origin with a visible vertical Y-axis of rotation
Dynamic 3D camera frustum showing how focal length 'f' physically changes the field of view
Projection lines connecting 3D scene points through the camera center to the sensor plane
A background plane of static 3D points that project onto the moving camera sensor
Equation overlay highlighting the dominating terms: (f + x^2/f) for horizontal and (xy/f) for vertical flow

interactions.label: Focal Length (f)
interactions.teaches: Demonstrates the transition from a highly curved, wide-angle motion field (small f) to a uniform, translation-like motion field (large f) during pure camera rotation.

interactions.label: Rotation Velocity (Wy)
interactions.teaches: Scales the amplitude of all vectors and reverses their direction when the sign of rotation changes, while leaving the geometric flow structure intact.

interactions.label: 3D Camera View
interactions.teaches: Toggles between a flat 2D projection-plane vector field and a 3D spatial perspective showing the camera frustum rotating relative to the world.

demo_steps.title: Standard focal length (f = 1.0)
demo_steps.narration: We begin with a standard focal length of f = 1.0. As the camera rotates around its vertical Y-axis, a characteristic flow pattern emerges: vectors at the center flow horizontally, but towards the top and bottom corners they curve outward due to the perspective projection equations.

demo_steps.title: Wide angle distortion (f = 1/3)
demo_steps.narration: Lower the focal length to f = 0.33. Now, the quadratic distortion terms (x²/f and xy/f) dominate. Notice how the vectors spray aggressively outward at the corners and the field looks highly non-uniform—this is typical of a wide-angle lens undergoing rotation.

demo_steps.title: Telephoto translation limit (f = 3.0)
demo_steps.narration: Increase the focal length to f = 3.0. In this telephoto setting, the quadratic distortion terms shrink toward zero because division by a large f suppresses them. The motion field becomes nearly uniform and horizontal, mimicking lateral camera translation.

demo_steps.title: Reversing camera rotation
demo_steps.narration: Let's reverse the camera's rotational direction by setting Wy to -1.0. Notice how the flow direction completely flips, yet the underlying geometric pattern remains perfectly identical. Rotation speed only scales and signs the flow, never changing the path shape.

demo_steps.title: The 3D physical explanation
demo_steps.narration: Switching to the 3D camera view reveals the physical setup. See how a shorter focal length creates a wide-angle frustum close to the projection center, forcing rays from the scene to hit the sensor at extreme, sweeping angles as the camera yaws.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Set camera to look directly down the Z-axis for the flat 2D vector field mode. When 3D Camera View is enabled, smoothly transition to a perspective viewpoint from the upper-right-front to visualize the camera body, its frustum, and the rotating Y-axis.
interactionPlan.notes: The 2D vectors should be calculated dynamically for each grid point (x, y) using the equations: dx = (f + x^2/f) * Wy and dy = (x*y/f) * Wy. When switching view modes, smoothly animate the camera position using a slerp to provide a seamless conceptual transition between 2D math and 3D geometry.

attempts.html:
Standard focal length (f = 1.0)
Standard focal length: vectors curve near the corners while the center remains mostly horizontal.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The original figure displays three panels side-by-side simultaneously for f=1/3, f=1, and f=3, whereas the generated figure only displays a single interactive panel.
The initial camera view is extremely zoomed out, making the vector grid look very tiny in the center of the screen.
The vector density is high and arrow sizes are small, making the detailed curving of vectors harder to see than in the clean, high-contrast original drawing.
Mathematical equations and descriptive labels overlay directly onto the plot area, cluttering the view compared to the clean original labels under each plot.
evaluation.notes: Excellent mathematical accuracy and interactive features, but fails to replicate the side-by-side presentation of the three focal lengths, and the default camera zoom is much too far away.
action_items: Adjust the initial camera zoom and position to frame the vector field much larger and centered on the screen.
Increase the size and thickness of the arrow helpers so the flow direction is highly visible.
Clean up the label placement and increase font sizes to prevent overlapping or tiny, unreadable text.
Consider presenting three small side-by-side 3D viewports or groups to mimic the original textbook's comparative structure directly.

feedback.rationale: The conceptual representation and interactivity are excellent (scores of 5), but the execution needs concrete visual refinements including fixing the camera zoom, increasing arrow sizes, and improving label placement.
action_items: Adjust the initial camera zoom and position to frame the vector field much larger and centered on the screen.

Clean up the label placement and increase font sizes to prevent overlapping or tiny, unreadable text.
Consider presenting three small side-by-side 3D viewports or groups to mimic the original textbook's comparative structure directly.

attempts.html:
Standard focal length (f = 1.0)
The flat view recreates the textbook comparison: left f=1/3, middle interactive f, right f=3.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The UI buttons and narration text box heavily overlap and obscure the left vector field panel (f=1/3)
The 3D arrows used in the flow fields are thicker and blockier compared to the thin, elegant 2D lines in the original figure
The grid density and arrow spacing differ slightly from the original textbook representation
evaluation.notes: Excellent mathematical and conceptual translation with great interactive features, but the UI elements heavily overlap and clutter the left panel of the 2D visualization.
action_items: Reposition the UI controls and narration text into a dedicated, non-overlapping sidebar or a compact top banner to prevent obscuring the vector fields
Refine the ArrowHelper parameters (shaft diameter, head length, head width) to make the arrows thinner and more elegant, matching the textbook style
Add more padding between the three panels (left, middle, right) to give the visualization room to breathe

feedback.rationale: The conceptual model and interactivity are excellent, but execution-level fixes are needed to resolve UI overlaps, improve panel spacing, and refine the arrow aesthetics.

attempts.html:
Standard focal length (f = 1.0)
The flat view recreates the textbook comparison: left f=1/3, middle interactive f, right f=3.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The vector field arrows are extremely thick and heavily crowded/overlapping compared to the thin, clean arrows in the source figure.
In the f=3 panel, the oversized arrowheads merge into thick solid lines, whereas the original has distinct, well-spaced horizontal arrows.
The generated figure includes an extra mathematical equation label above the middle panel that is not present in the original textbook image.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive functionality and 3D mode, but the 2D vector fields are visually cluttered and over-scaled compared to the elegant source drawing.
action_items: Reduce the grid density (e.g., from 13x13 to 9x9 or 10x10) to prevent arrow crowding and overlap.
Decrease the arrow helper shaft diameter and arrowhead dimensions (width/length) to match the thin line style of the original illustration.
Cap or scale down the maximum visual length of the arrows in the f=3 case so they don't overlap horizontally and form solid black bars.
Optionally make the equation label toggleable or smaller so it doesn't distract from the primary textbook layout.

feedback.rationale: The conceptual model and interactivity are excellent, but the visual execution of the vector field arrows (scale, density, and thickness) needs refinement to prevent crowding and match the source's clean appearance.
